section.title: Introduction
section.para: Text dumps carry binaries: web pages inline images as data: URIs, documents paste base64 payloads, and hostile bodies smuggle encoded content. The package opennlp.tools.assets finds such assets as exact spans over the original text, identifies their format, and can replace them with text while keeping every offset mapped to the source.

Identification needs no decoding pass: when a payload starts at a file's first byte, the file magic has a fixed base64 image, so detection is literal matching inside base64 runs. A PNG payload always starts iVBORw0KGgo, a JPEG /9j/, a GIF R0lGOD, a RIFF container UklGR, a PDF JVBERi0, and a zip archive UEsDB. The built-in table ranges across image, document, archive, audio, video, font, database, certificate, and executable formats, PNG to Parquet to PEM-armored keys; the magic values are derived from the leading bytes each format's specification publishes and from the mime-type catalog of Apache Tika. Only a magic anchored at the first byte can be scanned for this way, so a container whose signature sits at a later offset, such as the ISO base media format of MP4, is out of scope for bare runs. Signatures that classify loose prose rather than a file, such as mail header lines or source-code conventions, are deliberately absent. A bare run is only reported when it is long enough to be a real payload, carries such a prefix, and its decoded leading bytes verify the magic; a data: URI declares its media type and is reported at any payload length. Decoding the leading bytes also yields image dimensions where the format's header carries them.

section.title: Detecting and decoding
Folding assets into text
section.para:
AssetAnnotator provides the opennlp:assets layer, and AssetFolder replaces every asset with text. The result is an AlignedText: spans over the folded text map back to exact original offsets, so downstream findings over the cleaned text still locate in the raw input. The default replacement renders a caption from the asset's own metadata:
Document document = new AssetAnnotator().annotate(Document.of(text)); AlignedText folded = AssetFolder.fold(document, AssetFolder.caption()); // folded.normalizedString() = "An image [png 5x7, 45 bytes] follows."
Mapping the caption back recovers the asset's exact span in the original: folded.toOriginalSpan(9, 28), the caption's offsets in the folded text, returns the span of the whole data URI.

What replaces an asset is the caller's choice through the replacement function. A BinaryContentDescriber, the seam for external binary understanding such as document text extraction or image description, composes in directly:
BinaryContentDescriber describer = ...; // supplied by the caller
AlignedText described = AssetFolder.fold(document, annotation -> describer.describe(annotation.value().decode(text), annotation.value().mediaType()));
The interface takes bytes and a media type and returns text. Implementations are constructor-injected, so integrating one adds no dependency to this toolkit; any dependency injection container can register an implementation as a bean unchanged.
